topic: Area of Study 3: Developments in Instrumental Jazz 1910 to Present
subject: music

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.

items.q: What happens in the recapitulation of sonata form?
items.correct: Both subjects return, but the second subject is now in the tonic key.
items.why: Students miss the KEY RESOLUTION. The exposition put subject 2 in a contrasting key; the recapitulation brings it back to the tonic, resolving the tonal tension - this is the dramatic point of sonata form.

items.q: How does D Dorian differ from D minor?
items.correct: Dorian has a raised 6th (B natural) compared to natural minor.
items.random_wrong: Dorian has no sharps or flats at all.
items.why: Students treat modes as variants of major/minor. Dorian on D uses only white keys (like C major, but starting on D), giving a raised 6th compared to D natural minor - producing the modal flavour.

items.q: Which Miles Davis album popularised modal jazz?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Kind of Blue. “Bitches Brew” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which saxophonist is most associated with the development of bebop?
items.why: It's Charlie Parker. “John Coltrane” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Miles Davis's 'Kind of Blue' released?
items.why: The real figure is 1959. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
